prompt:
**Tomasulo + ROB with speculation.** Consider a Tomasulo machine with the following structure:
- **4 reservation stations (RS1–RS4)** feeding **2 load units (LDU1, LDU2)** with **latency 2 cycles**;
- **2 reservation stations (RS5, RS6)** feeding **2 FP units (FPU1, FPU2)** with **latency 3 cycles**;
- **3 reservation stations (RS7, RS8, RS9)** feeding **1 integer ALU (ALU1)** with **latency 1 cycle**;
- a **10-slot ROB (entries 0–9)**;
- **separate CDBs** for integer and floating-point operations.

The branch is **wrongly speculated as TAKEN**; the misprediction resolution takes **10 cycles on top of the ALU latency**.

**P1.A** List all the possible **true** data dependences and **control** dependences in the code considering **two iterations** executing.
**P1.B** Execute the **two iterations** of the code, according to the wrong-speculation assumption and the dependences highlighted before.
**P1.C** Report the structural details of the architecture (ROB slots, RS and FU numbers) in the table.

Fill the schedule table below for **both iterations** (14 rows). For each row give ISSUE / START EXE / EXE COMPLETE / WB / Commit and the **ROB idx / RSi / Unit** assigned to that instruction.

**How to fill ROB idx / RSi / Unit** (convention: always take the *lowest-numbered free* one):
- **ROB idx** = issue order: 1st issued → entry 0, 2nd → entry 1, …; entries are freed at commit and reused (wrap from 9 back to 0).
- **RSi** = first free reservation station *of the matching type* — loads/stores use the load stations (RS1–RS4), `FADD` uses the FP stations (RS5–RS6), `ADDUI`/`BNE` use the integer stations (RS7–RS9).
- **Unit** = a free functional unit of that type: LDU1/LDU2, FPU1/FPU2, ALU1.
- *Worked example:* `I0` issues first → **ROB 0**; it is a load → first load station **RS1** on **LDU1**. `I1` → **ROB 1, RS2, LDU2**. `I3` is the first `FADD` → **RS5** on **FPU1**.

Because the branch is mispredicted, the **second-iteration** instructions are fetched along the wrong path and end up **squashed** at branch resolution — for those rows put the speculative exec timing and mark **Commit = flushed**.

code.source:
FOR: LD    $F2, A($R1)   ; I0
     LD    $F4, B($R1)   ; I1
     LD    $F6, C($R1)   ; I2
     FADD  $F4, $F2, $F4 ; I3
     FADD  $F6, $F4, $F6 ; I4
     SD    $F6, A($R1)   ; I5
     ADDUI $R1, $R1, 8   ; I6
     BNE   $R1, $R2, FOR ; I7

fillTable.caption: P1.B / P1.C — two-iteration Tomasulo+ROB schedule (ISSUE / START EXE / EXE COMPLETE / WB / Commit) and ROB idx / RSi / Unit

solution:
**P1.A — dependences (two iterations).**
*True (RAW), within one iteration:*
- `$F2`: `I0` → `I3`
- `$F4`: `I1` → `I3`, and `I3` → `I4`
- `$F6`: `I2` → `I4`, and `I4` → `I5` (the store reads `$F6`)
- `$R1`: `I6` → `I7` (the branch tests the updated `$R1`)

*True, across iterations* (carried by `$R1` once `I6` writes it): `I6`(it1) → every use of `$R1` in it2, i.e. `I0`,`I1`,`I2`,`I5`,`I6`,`I7` of iteration 2. (The loads/store of it1 that read `$R1` *before* `I6` writes it are **anti / WAR** name dependences, removed by Tomasulo's renaming, not true dependences.)

*Control:* `I7` (`BNE`) controls **all** instructions of iteration 2 — they are fetched and issued only **speculatively**.

**P1.B — execution (model answer).** Issue is in-order, 1/cycle, when an RS of the right kind and a ROB slot are free; an op starts when its operands are on the CDB and an FU of that type is free; `EXE COMPLETE = START + latency − 1`; `WB` is the next cycle on the appropriate (int/FP) CDB; commit is in-order from the ROB head.

Iteration 1: the two loads `I0`/`I1` start at 2/3 (WB 4/5); `I2` waits for a free LDU → start 4, WB 6; `I3` `FADD` starts at 6 (after `$F2`,`$F4`) WB 9; `I4` starts at 10 (needs `I3`'s `$F4`) WB 13; the store `I5` gets `$F6` at 13 → completes 15, WB 16; `I6` `ADDUI` completes in cycle 8, WB 9; `I7` `BNE` reads `$R1` from `I6` (start 10) and **resolves only after 1 (ALU) + 10 (mispred) cycles**, i.e. EXE COMPLETE at cycle 20, WB 21, commit 22.

Because the branch was **predicted taken** but is actually *not-taken* at the exit, the processor speculatively issues iteration-2 instructions into the ROB (entries ROB8, ROB9, then wrapping to ROB0, ROB1, … as iteration-1 entries free at commit). Their speculative timings (issue 9, 10, 11, …; the `FADD`s chained through the renamed `$F2`/`$F4`/`$F6`) are shown in the table, **but when the misprediction is detected (~cycle 20–21) all speculative iteration-2 entries are flushed and none commit** — hence *Commit = flushed*. Only iteration 1 commits, in order, with the branch paying the 10-cycle penalty plus the squashed speculative work.

**P1.C — structure.** ROB = **10 entries (0–9)** filled in issue order and reused at commit; **4 load RS** feeding **2 load units** (2cc); **2 FP RS** feeding **2 FP units** (3cc); **3 integer RS** feeding **1 ALU** (1cc). With **separate integer/FP CDBs**, an integer result and an FP result can broadcast in the same cycle. The single ALU serialises the address-update / branch chain (`I6`→`I7`), and that chain plus the misprediction penalty bounds the iteration time. Iteration-1 assignment: `I0` ROB0/RS1/LDU1, `I1` ROB1/RS2/LDU2, `I2` ROB2/RS3/LDU1, `I3` ROB3/RS5/FPU1, `I4` ROB4/RS6/FPU2, `I5` ROB5/RS4/LDU2, `I6` ROB6/RS7/ALU1, `I7` ROB7/RS8/ALU1. Iteration-2 entries (ROB8, ROB9, then wrapping to ROB0, ROB1, …) are issued speculatively and **flushed** at the branch, so they never commit.

prompt:
**Scoreboard with extended physical register file.** Assume that the following code has been executed on a CPU with a **Scoreboard** with an **extended physical register file of 128 physical registers**. The PRF has no limitations and can accommodate a parallel infinite amount of reads and writes. The execution table below is **given**:

**P2.A** List all the possible **true** data dependences — and only them — in the code.
**P2.B** Is there a Scoreboard *configuration* (i.e., FU numbers and write ports) that can respect the shown execution? Yes or No? Explain why, and if yes, detail the architecture characteristics. Assume memory operations are unambiguous.
**P2.C** Considering the previous execution, report the **rename table** status at **cycle 8** and **cycle 15** (physical registers of 64 bits). Explain your choice and assumptions. The initial rename table at cycle 0 is: `$F0`→`P0`, `$F1`→`P2`, `$2`→`P130`.

fillTable.caption: Given execution (READ-ONLY): Scoreboard with 128-entry PRF — ISSUE / READ OPERAND / EXE COMPLETE / WB

solution:
**P2.A — true (RAW) dependences (and only those).**
- Block 1: `$f1`: `I1`→`I3`; `$f0`: `I2`→`I3`; `$f1`: `I4`→`I5`; `$f0`: `I3`→`I5`; `$f0`: `I5`→`I6` (store reads `$f0`).
- Block 2: `$f1`: `I7`→`I9`; `$f0`: `I8`→`I9`; `$f1`: `I10`→`I11`; `$f0`: `I9`→`I11`; `$f0`: `I11`→`I12`.
- Integer block: `$2`: `I13`→`I14`; `$2`: `I14`→`I15`.

Every *other* reuse of `$f0`/`$f1`/`$2` is a **WAW / WAR name dependence** — and these are **removed by the 128-entry PRF + renaming**. That is exactly why `I4` (`ld $f1`) can read/write at 5/7 without waiting for `I3` to finish with the old `$f1`, and why the two add/load groups overlap freely.

**P2.B — is there a configuration that respects this?** **Yes.** The implied FU latencies are read off as `EXE COMPLETE − READ OPERAND`:
- `ld` = **2cc** (`I1`: 4 − 2 = 2);
- `mul.d` = **3cc** (`I3`: 10 − 7 = 3);
- `add.d` = **3cc** (`I5`: 15 − 12 = 3);
- `addi` = **1cc** (`I14`: 21 − 20 = 1);
and `WB = EXE COMPLETE + 1` throughout.

A Scoreboard with **≥2 load/store units, ≥2 FP units (or one pipelined adder + one multiplier), and 1 integer ALU**, plus **enough write ports**, respects the table. Because the PRF is stated **unlimited (128 regs, infinite read/write ports)**, all WAW/WAR name conflicts on `$f0`/`$f1` are renamed away, so there are **no WAW issue stalls and no WAR write-back stalls and no write-port contention**. The only stalls left are the **RAW** ones: `I3.RO = 7` waits for `I2.WB = 6` (+1); `I5.RO = 12` waits for the multiply `I3.WB = 11` (+1); the second block (`I9`/`I11`) mirrors the first; and the integer chain `I13`→`I14`→`I15` is purely RAW. Every timing in the table is explained by latency + RAW, so such a configuration exists.

**P2.C — rename table at cycle 8 and cycle 15.** Renaming allocates a **fresh** physical register to each instruction's destination at issue; we track the *most recently issued* writer of each architectural register. Assume the free list hands out `P131, P132, P133, …` in issue order to the FP-writing instructions, and continues for the integer ones. Mapping the destinations: `I1`(`$f1`)→P131, `I2`(`$f0`)→P132, `I3`(`$f0`)→P133, `I4`(`$f1`)→P134, `I5`(`$f0`)→P135, `I7`(`$f1`)→P136, `I8`(`$f0`)→P137, `I9`(`$f0`)→P138, `I10`(`$f1`)→P139, `I11`(`$f0`)→P140, `I13`(`$2`)→P141 (`I6`,`I12`,`I15` are stores → no destination).

*At cycle 8* — instructions `I1`–`I8` have issued. Latest writer of `$f1` = `I7` (→P136), of `$f0` = `I8` (→P137), `$2` not yet written (still P130):

| Arch reg | `$F0` | `$F1` | `$2` |
|---|---|---|---|
| Phys reg | P137 (I8) | P136 (I7) | P130 |

*At cycle 15* — instructions `I1`–`I13` have issued. Latest writer of `$f1` = `I10` (→P139), of `$f0` = `I11` (→P140), of `$2` = `I13` (→P141):

| Arch reg | `$F0` | `$F1` | `$2` |
|---|---|---|---|
| Phys reg | P140 (I11) | P139 (I10) | P141 (I13) |

The exact physical numbers depend on the free-list policy; the load-bearing point is that **each redefinition maps the architectural register to a new physical register**, so the table at a given cycle reflects the most-recently *issued* writer of each name. (Model answer.)

prompt: **Branch prediction.** Describe (effectively supported) a **1-BHT** and a **2-BHT** able to execute the following assembly code. Is the result, in terms of mispredictions, in line with the theoretical characteristics of the two predictors? Effectively support your answer.

code.source:
li   t0, 0
outer_loop:
          li   t6, 2
          mul  t3, t0, t6
          li   t1, 0
inner_loop:
          add  t4, t0, t1
          addi t1, t1, 1
          li   t2, 100
          blt  t1, t2, inner_loop
          mul  t5, t0, t0
          addi t0, t0, 1
          li   t2, 1000
          blt  t0, t2, outer_loop

solution:
There are **two conditional branches**:
- the **inner** `blt t1, t2, inner_loop`: `t1` runs 1…100 against 100, so it is **taken 99 times and not-taken once** per inner episode (the inner loop body executes 100 times per entry);
- the **outer** `blt t0, t2, outer_loop`: `t0` runs 1…1000 against 1000, so it is **taken 999 times and not-taken once** over the whole run.

The inner loop is **entered 1000 times** (once per outer iteration), so the inner branch executes ≈ 100 × 1000 = 100 000 times total.

**1-bit BHT.** Each entry stores only the *last* outcome of the branch. For a loop branch this causes **two mispredictions per episode**: one at the **exit** (the predictor still says *taken* but the branch is *not-taken*) and one on the **first iteration of the next entry** (the predictor now says *not-taken* but the branch is *taken*). So the inner branch costs ≈ **2 mispredictions × 1000 episodes ≈ 2000** mispredictions; the outer branch, entered once, costs about **2** (one at its single exit, plus the initial cold prediction). Per inner episode of 100 branches that is 2/100 = 2% mispredict.

**2-bit BHT.** A **saturating 2-bit counter** needs *two* consecutive anomalies to flip its prediction. During the long loop body it saturates in *strongly-taken*; the single not-taken **exit** only nudges it to *weakly-taken*, so the prediction stays *taken* and **on re-entry the very first iteration is predicted correctly**. Each episode therefore costs **only 1 misprediction** (the exit). The inner branch costs ≈ **1 × 1000 ≈ 1000** mispredictions — roughly **half** the 1-bit count — and the outer branch costs about **1**.

**Is this in line with theory?** **Yes.** For regular, repeatedly-entered loops the 2-bit predictor pays **one** misprediction per episode versus **two** for the 1-bit one, because the extra hysteresis bit absorbs the isolated exit anomaly and protects the re-entry prediction. Halving the loop mispredictions (here ≈ 2000 → ≈ 1000) is exactly the textbook advantage of a 2-bit over a 1-bit BHT, so the measured result matches the predictors' theoretical characteristics. (Model answer.)
